title: UML Use Case Diagram — ATM System
examTip: In a UML Use Case diagram, identify actors as stick figures (represented as styled actorNode boxes in Mermaid) and use cases as ovals (styled as usecaseNode). Draw the system boundary enclosing only the use cases. Map relationship types accurately: use dotted arrows with <<include>> labels to show mandatory sub-tasks (like PIN verification before cash withdrawal).
theory:
A UML Use Case Diagram captures the functional requirements of a system by outlining the interactions between external actors and system-defined operations. For an Automated Teller Machine (ATM) system, this diagram outlines user roles and host communications.

Actors: External entities interacting with the ATM system. They include the Customer (who initiates transactions), the ATM Technician (who maintains the hardware and refills cash), and the Bank Host/Database (the server authorization unit that validates accounts and runs transaction ledger updates).

Use Cases inside the System Boundary: The system includes six key client-facing use cases: Check Account Balance, Withdraw Cash, Deposit Cash, Transfer Funds, Change PIN, and Authenticate User. It also contains administrative use cases like Refill Cash Tray and Perform Diagnostics.

Include Relationships: To check balance, withdraw, deposit, transfer, or change a PIN, the customer must first authenticate. Therefore, these five transactions have an <<include>> dependency pointing to the 'Authenticate User' usecase. This shows that PIN validation is a mandatory, reusable submodule of all primary transactions. Dotted arrows represent these associations, mapping out logical program dependencies for backend developers.
keyPoints: External actors (Customer, Technician, Bank Host) represent users or systems interacting with the ATM
The System Boundary defines what is inside the ATM application vs. what is external to it
Use cases represent the discrete operations (Withdraw, Deposit, Refill) provided by the system
An <<include>> relationship indicates a mandatory sub-action (like PIN verification) required by a parent use case
The Bank Host represents a secondary actor because it receives transaction queries but does not initiate them
useCases: Defining software requirements and user stories during the inception phase of banking apps
Guiding quality assurance testers to draft test cases covering all user-ATM interaction paths
Communicating system features and access roles to project managers and stakeholders
mermaid_code:
flowchart LR
  subgraph Actors["External Actors"]
    Customer["Customer Actor"]:::actorNode
    Technician["ATM Technician Actor"]:::actorNode
    BankHost["Bank Host and Authorization System"]:::actorNode
  end

  subgraph ATMSys["ATM System Boundary"]
    direction TB
    UC_Auth(["Authenticate User PIN"]):::usecaseNode
    UC_Check(["Check Account Balance"]):::usecaseNode
    UC_Withdraw(["Withdraw Cash"]):::usecaseNode
    UC_Deposit(["Deposit Cash"]):::usecaseNode
    UC_Transfer(["Transfer Funds"]):::usecaseNode
    UC_ChangePin(["Change PIN"]):::usecaseNode
    UC_Refill(["Refill Cash Tray"]):::usecaseNode
    UC_Maintain(["Perform Diagnostics"]):::usecaseNode
    
    %% Relationships
    UC_Check -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Auth
    UC_Withdraw -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Auth
    UC_Deposit -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Auth
    UC_Transfer -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Auth
    UC_ChangePin -.->|"<<include>>"| UC_Auth
  end

  Customer --> UC_Check
  Customer --> UC_Withdraw
  Customer --> UC_Deposit
  Customer --> UC_Transfer
  Customer --> UC_ChangePin

  Technician --> UC_Refill
  Technician --> UC_Maintain

  UC_Auth --> BankHost
  UC_Check --> BankHost
  UC_Withdraw --> BankHost
  UC_Deposit --> BankHost
  UC_Transfer --> BankHost
